coredata.dc:title: PCR- and label-free SARS-CoV-2 electrochemical immunosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Vásquez, Viviana
Orozco, Jahir
coredata.dc:description: Cases of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) are still ubiquitously persistent, as new variants have emerged worldwide. Furthermore, severity and infectiousness are still not fully understood. Therefore, an imperative need still exists for more and better viral detection and diagnosis tools. Nanostructured electrochemical biosensors have shown outstanding features for highly sensitive Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) detection and diagnosis. This work reports on an electrochemical immunosensor based on nanocomposite-decorated screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) for one-step detection of the spike protein by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) without the need for enzymatic or PCR amplification. The SPCEs were decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coexisting with gold and platinum nanoparticle nanocomposite (GQDs@AuPt), improving their conductive properties and offering a high surface area and available carboxylic groups for the covalent immobilization of capture recombinant antibodies. As a result, the biosensor demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity in detecting spike protein by DPV in a linear range from 0.05 to 1.5 µg/mL and with a LOD of 60.14 ng/mL (0.78 nM). In addition, its potential application was demonstrated by detecting eleven positive concerning four negative nasopharyngeal swab samples from patients with COVID-19 and healthy individuals previously analyzed by the RT-PCR gold standard.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SARS-CoV-2
Spike protein
Immunosensor
Coronavirus
Graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical biosensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and modification of electrodes with GQDs@AuPt

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge MinCiencias for funding the project Validation of a Nanobiosensor to detect SARS-CoV-2 rapidly (Cod. 111593092980). J.O acknowledges financial support from the Minciencias, the University of Antioquia, and the Max Planck Society through the Cooperation Agreement 566-1, 2014. The authors are thankful to the PECET from the University of Antioquia for donations of patient samples. And the authors also thank EPM and Ruta N for hosting the Max Planck Tandem Groups.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of A) GQDs, B) Au, and C) Pt concentrations in the biosensor assembly. The data for each point to the curves corresponds to the peak current (approximately at a potential of 0.1 V) in the DPV measurement of each concentration evaluated.

A) Immunosensor electrochemical response with increasing concentrations of 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 µg/mL of the spike protein recorded by DPV at 50 mV/s, in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-/KNO3 solution. B) Response curve and linear difference on the current correlation (Δ current) after and before (blocking with BSA) incubation with different concentrations of the spike protein. The linear equation that describes the behavior is Δi = 13.20 [spike protein] + 0.779.

Immunosensor specificity and selectivity when detecting 1 µg/mL spike, RBD and β-1,4-GALT-5 proteins and 1 × 106 copies/mL MERS-CoV, SARS-CoV, and SARS-CoV-2 viral particles. Statistically significant differences (a) to the negative control (NC) and (b) to the commercial spike protein, ***(p < 0.001), *(p < 0.05), and – (p > 0.05).

Immunosensor response of different lysed patient samples compared to a commercial spike protein-positive control and a negative control without the target protein. Blue columns correspond to negative samples by PCR. Green, purple, and cyan columns correspond to positive samples by PCR at high, medium, and low cycle thresholds (C.T). PC and NC are positive and negative controls in the presence and absence of spike protein, respectively (n = 3). Statistically significant differences concerning the negative samples (N), ***(p < 0.001), **(p < 0.01), *(p < 0.05) and – (p > 0,05). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Conceptual schematic of the immunosensor. SPCE is modified by electrodeposition with a solution containing GQDs, Au and Pt. The carboxyl groups from GQDs are activated with EDC/NHS, the anti-spike capture antibody is then conjugated, and the surface is blocked with 1 M ethanolamine (ETA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA). Finally, the spike protein is detected by incubating for 30 min and detection by DPV.
Overview of recent electrochemical biosensors for detecting the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MBs: Magnetic beads, CA: Chronoamperometry, SPAuE: screen-printed gold electrode, EIS: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy, SPCE: screen-printed carbon electrode, PABA: para-aminobenzoic acid, FET: Field-Effect Transistor, PDFT: Perfluorodecanethiol, SWV: square wave voltammetry, Au-TFME: thin-film metal electrodes, MIP: molecularly imprinted polymer, AuNPs, gold nanoparticles; FTO, fluorinated tin oxide; MB, methylene blue; GO, graphene oxide; CNF, carbon nanofibers; UiO-66, Universitetet i Oslo-66; MP-Au-SPE, macroporous gold screen-printed electrode; CNT, carbon nanotube; rGO: reduced graphene oxide, CP: chronopotentiometry, NRs, nanorods; Ni3(BTC)2 MOF, nickel-benzene tricarboxylic acid-metal–organic framework.

head.ce:title: PCR- and label-free SARS-CoV-2 electrochemical immunosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Max Planck Tandem Group in Nanobioengineering, Institute of Chemistry, Faculty of Natural and Exact Sciences, University of Antioquia, Complejo Ruta N, Calle 67 N° 52-20, Medellín 050010, Colombia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Max Planck Tandem Group in Nanobioengineering
Faculty of Natural and Exact Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Max Planck Tandem Group in Nanobioengineering, Institute of Chemistry, Faculty of Natural and Exact Sciences, University of Antioquia, Complejo Ruta N, Calle 67 N° 52-20, Medellín 050010, Colombia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cases of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) are still ubiquitously persistent, as new variants have emerged worldwide. Furthermore, severity and infectiousness are still not fully understood. Therefore, an imperative need still exists for more and better viral detection and diagnosis tools. Nanostructured electrochemical biosensors have shown outstanding features for highly sensitive Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) detection and diagnosis. This work reports on an electrochemical immunosensor based on nanocomposite-decorated screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) for one-step detection of the spike protein by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) without the need for enzymatic or PCR amplification. The SPCEs were decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coexisting with gold and platinum nanoparticle nanocomposite (GQDs@AuPt), improving their conductive properties and offering a high surface area and available carboxylic groups for the covalent immobilization of capture recombinant antibodies. As a result, the biosensor demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity in detecting spike protein by DPV in a linear range from 0.05 to 1.5 µg/mL and with a LOD of 60.14 ng/mL (0.78 nM). In addition, its potential application was demonstrated by detecting eleven positive concerning four negative nasopharyngeal swab samples from patients with COVID-19 and healthy individuals previously analyzed by the RT-PCR gold standard.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: COVID-19 is caused by SARS-CoV-2, which generated concern worldwide because it can produce severe symptoms (mainly respiratory failure) that can lead to death [1,2]. Therefore, rapid, simple, and specific virus detection is still essential, especially in detecting silent/asymptomatic infections. Detection is currently done by reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR), which detects the virus's genetic material but requires robust equipment and trained personnel, or by antigen testing, which detects the structural proteins of the virus, but often with cross-reactivity and low sensitivity [3]. In contrast, biosensors benefit from the unique properties of nanomaterials and the high affinity of bioreceptors for rapid, specific, and highly sensitive detection of target biomarkers, offering a promising solution to the limitations of conventional methods for SARS-CoV-2 detection [4–7]. In addition, electrochemical biosensors are particularly attractive because they can be designed with different geometries [8], modified with composites [9,10], and due to their amenability to miniaturize, offering portability and, thus, new opportunities for point-of-care detection solutions [11].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a new zero-dimensional nanomaterial [12], derived from organic compounds [13], by diverse straightforward and low-cost synthesis methods and mainly known for their unique fluorescence, quantum confinement, and crystal structure properties [14]. GQDs also enjoy photoluminescence, high stability in aqueous solution, high biocompatibility, and high content of functional groups that can provide abundant binding sites to link specific bioreceptors. Additionally, they have rich and high surface areas and fast electron conduction in (bio)sensing interfaces, which is desirable for developing highly sensitive electrochemical biosensors [14]. Therefore, GQDs have been incorporated into electrochemical biosensors to detect dopamine, alpha-fetoprotein, carcinoembryonic antigen, D2 vitamin [14] and cephalexin [15] among many other target analytes.

Noble metals have been incorporated into biosensing formats due to their enhanced physicochemical properties that depend on sizes, such as quantum confinement and surface plasmon resonance, high surface area, and due to synthesis simplicity [16]. Silver and gold nanoparticles are the most widely studied because they act as immobilizing platforms, accelerating electron transfer and catalysis. In electrochemical biosensors, metal nanoparticles have been used as labels or to increase the loading of electroactive species [17,18].

Nanocomposites have demonstrated to enhance the properties of transducers and electrode interfaces, including increasing surface area, favoring electron transfer, and incorporating bioreceptors, among others. In this context, nanocomposites from metallic nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, graphene, metal oxide nanostructures, and conductive polymers have been the most reported [19,20]. Furthermore, enhanced properties of nanocomposites based on GQDs and metal nanoparticles have been used in synergy to develop electrochemical (bio)sensors [21]. For instance, GQDs have demonstrated increasing the chemical reactivity and decreasing the oxidation potential of AuNPs and AgNPs, providing excellent electronic, optical, catalytic, and analytical properties with high stability and reproducibility of the resultant devices [22]. Furthermore, Pd-Au@GQDs nanocomposites have also been reported to detect colitoxin DNA, with excellent electrochemical properties [23] and an α-MnO2/GQDs nanocomposite for quantifying glucose and L-tyrosine and detecting diabetes and diabetic foot ulcer using a micro-fluidic platform [24]. In addition, Au-GQDs@AgPt nanostructures have been designed as an electrocatalytic agent for methanol oxidation [25].

In this article, we combine the excellent properties of GQDs and noble metals to design a PCR- and label-free immunosensor to detect SARS-CoV-2 spike protein. GQDs were synthesized by a straightforward bottom-up approach [22], and GQDs@AuPt nanocomposites were prepared by electrodeposition from a solution of GQDs and the Au and Pt precursor salts [26]. An anti-spike antibody attached to the GQDs@AuPt-decorated SPCE by covalent coupling with carbodiimide. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, fluorescence, energy dispersion X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterized the formation of GQDs, GQDs@AuPt, and the decorated SPCE. The immunosensor was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and the response was evaluated by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). This proof of concept demonstrates the potential of biosensors as tools for the rapid and specific detection of SARS-CoV-2, but it holds, the potential for detecting other emerging viruses.

SARS-CoV-2 (2019-nCoV) spike antibody, rabbit PAb (Ref. 40591-T62); SARS-CoV-2 (2019-nCoV) spike S1-His recombinant protein (Ref. 40591-V08H) were obtained from Sino Biological. N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) (Ref. E6383-5G), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) 98% (Ref. 130672-5G) and 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid sodium salt (MES) 99% (M3885-100G), gold three chloride trihydrate 99.9% (Ref. 520918-1G) and chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate 37.5% (Ref. 206083-1G), were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium hydrogen phosphate 99.0% (K2HPO4) and disodium hydrogen phosphate 99.0% (Na2HPO4) were acquired from PanReac AppliChem. Potassium chloride 99.0% (KCl) and sodium chloride 99.5% (NaCl) were obtained from J.T. Baker®. Potassium hexacyanoferrate trihydrate 98.5% (K4[Fe(CN)6]*3H2O), and potassium ferricyanide 99% (K3[Fe(CN)6]) were purchased from Merck Millipore (Darmstadt, Germany). Sulfuric acid 0.5 M 99.9% (H2SO4) was purchased from Honeywell FlukaTM (Seelve, Germany), and citric acid 99.5% (CAS 77–92-9) was purchased from VWR Chemicals.

25 mM MES buffer pH 6.5 was used to activate carboxylic groups and conjugate anti-spike antibodies. In addition, 0.15 M phosphate buffer 1X pH 7.4 (PB) was used to conjugate the spike protein. ChemCruz radioimmunoprecipitation lysis buffer (RIPA, Ref. sc-24948), containing 1% phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF), 1% sodium orthovanadate, and 2% protease inhibitor cocktail, was used for lysing viral particles and samples.

The electrochemical measurements were performed with a three-electrode cell configuration SPCE (Ref. 110BT, from DropSens) in a PalmSens4 potentiostat with a PS Trace software analyzer. The chips consist of a 4 mm carbon working electrode, a carbon counter electrode, and a silver pseudo-reference electrode, printed on the same strip.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and modification of electrodes with GQDs@AuPt
ce:section.ce:para: The protocol for synthesizing GQDs was described by Álvarez-Diduk [27]. Briefly, 1 g of citric acid was brought at 200 °C for approximately 40 min, or until the solution changed from clear to orange, at which point it was transferred to 50 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution, and homogenized for 5 min, then pH was adjusted to 7.2 with 0.1 M HCl, filtered through 0.2 µm filter and stored at 4 °C. Next, GQDs@AuPt nanostructures were obtained by electrodeposition at SPCEs. For this, SPCEs were activated by CV in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution in a potential window from + 1.6 V to 0 V at 0.1 V/s sweep rate for 10 cycles. Then the electrode surface was cleaned with a 1X PB buffer by CV in a potential window from −1.2 V to + 1.0 V at 0.1 V/s sweep rate for 5 cycles. Subsequently, a solution containing 0.5 mg/mL GQDs, 2 mM HAuCl4·3H2O, and 0.25 mM H2PtCl6·6H2O in water was added and electrodeposited by swiping the potential 15 times in a potential window from + 1.0 V to −0.7 V and at 0.05 V/s sweep rate.

GQDs or GQDs@AuPt solution were added to a 96-well plate, and the absorbance spectrum was measured between 300 and 800 nm in a Thermo Scientific™ Varioskan™ LUX. Similarly, the fluorescence spectrum was measured between 385 and 750 nm and an excitation wavelength of 365 nm in the same equipment.

The morphology and the semi-quantitative chemical composition analysis of the modified SPCEs were analyzed by SEM with a gold coating on a JEOL-JSM 6490LV microscope 20 kV accelerating voltage and equipped with an OXFORD INCAPentaFET- × 3 electron microprobe for EDX microanalysis.

The hydrodynamic size of the GQDs was characterized by DLS using the Malvern particle size analyzer (Zetasizer Pro Blue Label, Malvern Instruments). The presence of Au and Pt and the effect of GQDs on the nanocomposite was characterized by CV in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution in a + 1.6 V to + 0.2 V potential window at 50 mV/s sweep rate. In addition, chronoamperometry, at a fixed potential of −0.125 V in 10 mM H2O2, was used for the same purpose.

The carboxylic groups of the GQDs@AuPt nanostructures on the modified electrodes were activated in a 400 mM/100 mM EDC/NHS in 25 mM MES buffer pH 6.5 solution for 30 min. Subsequently, the anti-spike antibody was conjugated for 2 h in 25 mM MES buffer pH 6.5. The carboxylic groups were blocked first with a 1.0 M ethanolamine solution in 1X PB pH 8.0 for 15 min and then with a 0.5% BSA solution in PBS for 1 h. For the detection of the spike protein, it was incubated for 30 min in PB-BSA 1%.

The electrochemical biosensor was characterized at each step of the assembly by CV in a potential window from + 0.4 V to −0.2 V, by EIS at a frequency range of 50 kHz to 0.05 Hz and 0.010 V amplitude, and by DPV in a potential window from −0.3 V to + 0.3 V at 50 mV/s sweep rate. All measurements were performed on a 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4-/KNO3 redox probe.

The Tropical Diseases Study and Control Program (PECET - by its Spanish acronym) laboratory from the Universidad de Antioquia kindly donated nasopharyngeal swab samples. They were inactivated by UV-light for analysis by RT-PCR in a BSL-2 facility following established ethical and biosafety standards, following the RT-PCR protocol described elsewhere [28]. Briefly, samples obtained by nasopharyngeal swabs were immediately deposited in a transport medium, followed by RNA extraction. The amplification reaction was performed following the Berlin protocol, with some modifications [28]. Primers to detect the E gene and primers and a probe for the human RNAsaP gene were used as a control. In addition, a negative control in which water was added to replace the sample was included in all runs. RT-PCR reactions were performed on a BioRad thermocycler, CFX96, using 55 °C for 10 min for reverse transcription, followed by 95 °C for 3 min and then 45 cycles at 95 °C for 15 s and 58 °C for 30 s. Extracts were frozen at −80 °C until use.

For testing the samples with the immunosensor, the non-inactivated nasopharyngeal swabs were progressively thawed and lysed in 1/10 RIPA buffer dilution by vortexing for 1 min and 30 s, followed by sonication for 1 min in a BSL-2 laboratory following established biosafety and ethical standards.

Statistical analysis on the specificity of results and evaluation of patient samples used R studio software. Data were analyzed by the variance method (ANOVA) and compared the samples using the least significant difference (LSD) and Tukey method with a confidence level of 95 %. *** indicates a significance level p<0.001, ** values between 0.001 and 0.01, * values between 0.01 and 0.05, and higher than 0.05.

Nanomaterials have been implemented in developing biosensor platforms conferring numerous enhanced properties for detecting different biomarkers. Nanocomposites of GQDs and noble metals have attracted attention in electrochemical biosensors due to the synergistic effect of these materials to provide high surface area, excellent conductivity, biocompatibility, and catalytic activity, thereby promoting electron transfer and improving sensing performance [29]. Therefore, outstanding properties of a nanocomposite consisting of GQDs and a noble metal combination of Au and Pt were selected to develop a PCR- and amplification-free electrochemical immunosensor for detecting the spike protein from SARS-CoV-2 in a single step. As shown in Scheme 1 , GQDs@AuPt are electrodeposited on SPCE, the carboxyl groups of the GQDs are activated to covalent binding anti-spike antibodies, and subsequently, the remaining surface is blocked to avoid unspecific interactions. Finally, the detection of the spike protein by DPV is achieved without any amplification but with enough sensitivity for a clinical application.

As a first step, GQDs were synthesized from the thermal degradation of citric acid as an organic precursor (see Figure S1). This method allows for controlling the composition and physical properties of the GQDs [30], forming nanocrystals with abundant –OH, C = O and –COOH groups at the surface [31]. The GQDs showed a hydrodynamic size of 3.36 nm by DLS and an absorbance peak at 350–400 nm wavelength by UV–visible spectroscopy (see Figure S2-A). The peak is due to the sp2 clusters in the GQDs, absent in citric acid that shows absorbance below 250 nm [32]. Furthermore, GQDs exhibited a maximum emission peak at 465 nm upon excitation with a wavelength of 365 nm by fluorescence spectroscopy (see Figure S2-B), in agreement with reports in the literature [27].

After synthesis and characterization, GQDs in a gold and platinum salt solution were electroplated at SPCEs (see Figure S1). The solution was previously characterized by UV–visible spectrometry, showing a defined peak at 540 nm (see Figure S2-A), typical of Au surface plasmon resonance, evidencing the formation of spherical gold nanostructures with an approximate size of 50 nm [33]. The nanocomposite formation occurs later by reducing gold and platinum salts from the hydroxyl (–OH) groups on the GQDs surface, plus electrodeposition [23]. Therefore, an almost total decrease in the fluorescence of the GQDs was observed (see Figure S2-B) due to the GQDs turning off, produced by energy transfer when binding to metal ions [34]. The formation of embedded spherical nanostructures from the nanocomposite with 50–200 nm size was observed by SEM at the modified electrode, in agreement with spectrophotometric results (see Figure S2-C). Furthermore, Au- and Pt-specific peaks were observed, homogeneously distributed with C, and some Cl peaks were observed, which may be from the precursor salts (see Figure S2-D), Pt-, Au- and C were too, judged by mapping the surface of the modified working electrode by EDX (see Figure S2-E-H).

The concentrations of each electrodeposited nanocomposite component were optimized and characterized by CV, EIS, and DPV in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. First, decreasing concentrations of GQDs were evaluated (see Fig. 1 -A). Therefore, a 0.5 mg/mL concentration of GQDs was selected to achieve a high current but with enough carboxylic groups for further antibody anchorage. Subsequently, the concentration of the gold salt was changed from 0 to 10.0 mM, producing a current increase up to a plateau at 2.0 mM (see Fig. 1-B), selected as the optimal concentration. Finally, the concentration of the platinum salt was optimized with concentrations between 0 and 5.0 mM, where the highest current signal was obtained at a concentration of 0.25 mM (see Fig. 1-C).

The nanocomposite-modified SPCE was then electrochemically characterized to show the presence of metallic structures on top, the improved catalytic response as we modified the SPCE, and elucidated the role of the components on the resultant electrochemical performance. For this purpose, each component was electrodeposited at the SPCE alone or in combination with another component. The response was evaluated and compared with that obtained from the GQDs@AuPt nanocomposite-modified SPCE. Subsequently, the reduction of 10 mM H2O2 solution was catalyzed by Pt in the nanocomposite, as evidenced by chronoamperometry at −0.125 V for 100 s, showing the advantage of the nanocomposite over the components alone (see Figure S3-A). Furthermore, the characteristic reduction peak of Au at + 0.6 V observed in sulphuric acid demonstrated the presence of Au from the electroplated nanocomposite (Figure S3-B). In addition, GQDs intensified the signal, demonstrating their potential as a reducing agent within the system and improving electron transfer.

The SPCEs were characterized in the presence of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-/KNO3 redox probe by CV (see Figure S3-C) and used together with the Randels-Sevick equation to calculate the electroactive area as the electrodes were modified (35) (Equation S2), i.e., 13.0, 15.8, 15.3, 15.1 and 16.4 mm2, for the SPCE, Au, AuPt, GQDs@Au, and GQDs@AuPt, respectively (see Table S1). These results indicate that modifying electrodes by combining different nanomaterials increases the roughness and surface area to immobilize more bioreceptors [35] and favors the charge transfer between the electrochemical probe and the electrode surface.

In the next step, the anodic and cathodic current peaks were analyzed by CV at different sweep rates (between + 0.005 and + 0.5 V) to establish whether the electrochemical reaction is controlled by diffusion or adsorption [35]. It was observed that the peak current increased linearly as the square root of the sweep speed increased (see Figure S4-A and S4-B), consistent with a diffusion-controlled redox process, and the presence of both anodic and cathodic peaks indicates a quasi-reversible behavior [35].

After modification of the SPCE with GQDs@AuPt, the anti-spike antibody was linked to the activated available carboxylic groups of the GQDs by carbodiimide covalent coupling, providing specificity and high sensitivity to the nanoimmunosensing device. CV, DPV, and EIS characterized each biosensor modification step (Figure S5). The final current signal correlated with the detection event was measured by DPV and dropped with increasing concentration of spike protein.

The blocking agent, blocking time, and the capture antibody concentration was optimized in the following experiments. First, the signals of positive and negative controls (with and without spike protein) were evaluated. Next, 1.0 M ETA, 0.5% BSA, 0.5% casein, ethanolamine plus 0.5% BSA, and ethanolamine plus 0.5% BSA, but the spike protein solution containing 1% BSA was evaluated. A higher signal-to-noise ratio was observed in the last-mentioned case (see Figure S6-A), selected as optimal. When testing the incubation time of 0.5% BSA for surface blocking, 15, 30, 45, and 60 min were evaluated, observing the best signal-to-noise ratio with 60 min of incubation (see Figure S6-B). Finally, the concentration of the anti-spike antibody was optimized, evaluating concentrations between 12.5 and 200 µg/mL, showing the best response at a concentration of 25.0 µg/mL (see Figure S6-C). Therefore, the optimal conditions were 25.0 µg/mL of anti-spike antibody, blocking with 1.0 M ETA for 15 min and 0.5% BSA for 60 min, and incubating the spike protein in a 1% BSA solution, used in the following experiments.

The specificity was studied by interrogating the signal of the commercial spike protein compared with that from the RBD of this protein and SARS-CoV-2 viral particles lysed with RIPA buffer (see Fig. 3 ), showing statistically significant differences concerning the negative control ***(p < 0.001). In addition, the interaction of the nanoimmunosensor was evaluated in the presence of SARS-CoV and MERS-CoV viral particles and with the β1,4-GALT-5 glycoprotein, showing statistically significant differences concerning the spike protein positive control ***(p < 0.001). Furthermore, the nanoimmunosensor differentially detected SARS-CoV-2 viral particles in a cocktail containing SARS-CoV and MERS-CoV viral particles and the β1,4-GALT-5 glycoprotein (see Fig. 3 green bar), with no statistically significant differences concerning the signal from SARS-CoV-2 viral particles – (p > 0.05). Overall, the results showed high specificity and selectivity of the device in detecting SARS-CoV-2. Finally, the inter-electrode reproducibility of the nanoimmunosensor was evaluated, showing a variation of < 7% among the signals resulting from the detection with five different electrodes. And the stability of the electrode modified with the antibodies was 20 days at 4 °C in a PB buffered solution, which is necessary to maintain the functionality of the antibody.

The electrochemical immunosensor was evaluated against samples of nasopharyngeal swabs from patients and compared with that from healthy individuals tested before by RT-PCR. This molecular technique provides information on the viral loading of the samples, classified into three levels, according to the C.T at which amplification begins, i.e., three negative samples (C.T over 35), four samples with low viral loading (C.T between 30 and 35), three samples with medium viral loading (C.T between 20 and 30) and four samples with high viral loading (C.T between 12 and 20). For their analysis, the samples were previously lysed with RIPA buffer at a 1/10 dilution based on previous protocols [28]. In addition, to analyze the results obtained after measuring the samples, it was necessary to adjust the signal by subtracting the baseline, which was displaced by 200 µA due to the matrix effect of both the sample medium and the RIPA buffer, based on preliminary studies.

As shown in Fig. 4 , when performing a statistical analysis of the values obtained by each sample, it was observed that one of the samples with high C.T, called H7, did not present statistically significant differences (p > 0.05) with the negative samples, this may be because it presented a high C.T of 34.08. Yet, the other samples with high C.T. did show a significant difference with a p < 0.05. Furthermore, it was also possible to observe a difference in the signal between the samples with high, medium, and low C.T, with a proportional increase of the signal and maintaining a statistically significant difference with the negative samples (p < 0.01 for M33 and L7 and p < 0.001 for the other samples with medium and low C.T). Based on the results, differential detection of 90% of the positive samples concerning the negative ones was possible. It demonstrates the immunosensor's detection capacity, even in samples with high C.T, without PCR or enzymatic amplification, and opens the path to implementation at the point of care.

The excellent properties of an SPCE decorated with a nanocomposite based on GQDs, Au, and Pt nanoparticles allowed the development of an electrochemical nanoimmunosensor for the PCR- and label-free detection of spike protein in nasopharyngeal samples. The outstanding conductivity, high surface area, and numerous functional groups of the nanocomposite favored the anti-spike antibody anchorage, fast electron transfer, and single-step detection of the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, with a sensitivity of 13.2 µA*mL/µg and a LOD of 60.14 ng/mL. In addition, the detection capability of the immunosensor was demonstrated by discriminating lysed samples from patients concerning negative samples from healthy individuals. The sensitive, specific, stable, and reproducible nanoimmunosensor of a single-use can be tailored on demand to detect not only SARS-CoV-2 but other emerging diseases, offering opportunities to be easily implemented at the point of care to screen infected patients/healthy individuals rapidly.

Viviana Vásquez: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jahir Orozco: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge MinCiencias for funding the project Validation of a Nanobiosensor to detect SARS-CoV-2 rapidly (Cod. 111593092980). J.O acknowledges financial support from the Minciencias, the University of Antioquia, and the Max Planck Society through the Cooperation Agreement 566-1, 2014. The authors are thankful to the PECET from the University of Antioquia for donations of patient samples. And the authors also thank EPM and Ruta N for hosting the Max Planck Tandem Groups.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109121.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure, Function, and Antigenicity of the SARS-CoV-2 Spike Glycoprotein
